behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response
behaviors.justification: Whether the LLM produces context-aware references vs. generic responses depends on real LLM behavior. A unit or integration test with a mocked LLM would always pass (mock returns specific references). A system test with mocked LLM would also pass. Only a real LLM call reveals if it actually references specific details from the thread or defaults to generic responses. This is non-deterministic behavior that varies with model capability and prompt quality.
behaviors.test_description: Call the draft generation with a real LLM on a multi-turn email thread containing specific facts (e.g., 'customer complaint about widget X failing on March 15'). Verify the generated draft mentions the specific fact, product name, and timeframe rather than generic phrases like 'I understand your concern' without references.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test mocking the LLM would always pass. An Integration test still mocking the LLM would pass. Only real LLM execution can reveal if context-awareness fails.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base
behaviors.justification: The consistency checker must be wired with the real knowledge base structure and real draft text. A unit test of the checker logic alone (isolated function) would miss integration failures: incorrect claim extraction, KB lookup failures, format mismatches between extracted claims and KB schema, or missed contradictions due to how claims are normalized. Integration test wires together the draft, consistency checker, and KB data to catch these real failures. (Note: If the checker itself is LLM-based, this becomes Agentic.)
behaviors.test_description: Generate a draft containing a factual claim (e.g., 'Our API response time is 50ms SLA'). Populate the knowledge base with a contradicting entry (e.g., 'API SLA is 100ms'). Run the consistency checker on draft + KB. Verify the checker flags the contradiction. Repeat with multiple claim types (dates, names, numbers) to ensure extraction and matching work correctly.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test of the checker in isolation would miss bugs in claim extraction, KB query formatting, or how the draft is preprocessed. Integration test wires the real components together.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on how the real LLM responds when the knowledge base is empty or irrelevant. An LLM might hallucinate facts, include a disclaimer, or produce a hedged response. Unit or Integration tests with a mocked LLM would always pass (mock always returns disclaimer). A System test still mocking the LLM would pass. Only a real LLM call with an empty KB reveals if the model actually includes a disclaimer or fabricates. Different models and temperatures will behave differently.
behaviors.test_description: Call the draft generation with a real LLM on an email thread that asks about a topic not covered in the knowledge base (e.g., 'What is your policy on X?' where X has no KB entry). Run with an empty or irrelevant KB. Verify the draft either explicitly disclaims lack of information ('I don't have information on this', 'I cannot confirm', etc.) or requests escalation, rather than inventing facts.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking the LLM defeats the test. Only real LLM behavior reveals fabrication risk.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic control-flow logic: if approval is not granted, the send function should not be called. A unit test of the approval state machine can verify this invariant without requiring real network calls or external services. The failure (draft sent before approval) would be caught by verifying the email send function is never invoked when approval is false or pending. Mocking the email service is appropriate here; we are testing the workflow logic, not the email delivery.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft and attempt to send it without triggering explicit user approval. Verify the send operation is blocked. Check that a mocked email service is never called. Then grant explicit approval and verify the send operation proceeds and the mocked email service is called exactly once.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test of the state machine logic is sufficient. Approval is a deterministic control-flow decision, not dependent on external systems or LLM behavior. Integration test is more robust but Unit is the true minimum.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread
behaviors.justification: This is a multi-step pipeline with multiple agentic dependencies: LLM draft generation (Agentic) and possibly LLM-based factual checking (Agentic), sequenced together. The failure could be at any step (draft generation fails, fact check fails, approval UI doesn't appear) or in the interactions between steps (output of step 1 is malformed for step 2). Integration testing with mocked LLMs would miss real LLM failures. System testing might not test the coordination. Only a Workflow test with real agentic steps in sequence captures the end-to-end behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real multi-turn email thread through the complete pipeline: LLM reads thread and generates draft, fact checker validates the draft against the real knowledge base, draft is presented to a simulated user approval interface. Verify: (1) draft is generated and is non-empty, (2) fact-check completes without error, (3) draft is presented for approval, (4) approval UI blocks sending until explicit approval, (5) on approval, draft is queued or sent. Repeat with threads containing conflicts with KB to ensure fact-check catches them.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test with mocked LLM would pass even if real LLM fails. System test might mock the LLM. Workflow test with real agentic calls in sequence is the true minimum for end-to-end verification.
